Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd try to set aside time on weekends, but with work and other stuff going on, it rarely stuck. I found myself cramming a lot, doing marathon sessions when assignments were due rather than spreading things out. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often spend time doing practical exercises rather than reading, which probably looked different from what others were doing.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd be on top of things, logging in regularly, but then something would come up—extra shifts at work or personal stuff—and I'd disappear for a bit. Deadlines were really what drove me to buckle down. I wish I could say I had a consistent schedule, but that just wasn't my reality.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or getting a notification about something new being posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I'd missed. If I'm being real, guilt was a big motivator too—that nagging feeling that I should be doing something. The mindfulness exercises I started doing helped me recognize that anxiety driving me to check in.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in felt normal, like during the first few weeks when everything was new and I was trying to stay on track. But it never really became automatic for me. I'd get into a groove, then life would interrupt, and I'd have to rebuild that habit. It felt like constantly starting over.
Q5: The video content was actually pretty helpful because I could watch it while doing other things or replay parts I didn't get. Discussion posts were useful too because seeing how other students understood things gave me different perspectives. Anything hands-on or interactive felt more worthwhile to me since I learn better by doing than by reading walls of text.
Q6: I definitely skipped a lot of the supplementary readings and optional materials. With limited time and resources, I had to prioritize what would directly impact my grades. Some of the longer documents just felt overwhelming, especially when I was already stressed. If something didn't seem immediately relevant to an assignment, it went to the bottom of my list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to what I needed to submit or if it helped something click in my brain. Practical applications mattered more to me than theory. If I could finish something and feel like I actually learned a skill or understood a concept better, that felt valuable. Abstract stuff that seemed disconnected from real outcomes? Not so much.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're juggling multiple courses, work, and life with limited resources, you have to be strategic. Required stuff got done because it had to. Optional things, even if interesting, felt like extras for students who had more time and less on their plate. There was always this guilt about not doing them though.
Q9: Yeah, absolutely. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs to my phone so I could study during bus rides or breaks at work. I also used YouTube videos and free resources online when the course materials didn't click for me. Sometimes I'd work through problems on paper, completely offline. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was studying from printed notes or working through problems by hand that would never show up in any system. I'd also discuss concepts with a friend who was taking similar courses—we'd meet up and work through stuff together. That collaborative learning was huge for me but completely invisible online. My brain works better when I'm physically doing something.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, then spend hours with it offline. My internet at home isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab what I needed and work with it later. One login to download something might represent days of actual study time. The LMS would just show that single access, not the work that followed.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety was through the roof, and I felt like I was drowning. Work got busier, assignments piled up across all four courses, and I just couldn't keep up. There were days I'd stare at the screen feeling paralyzed, unable to start anything. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me push through some of that, surprisingly.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the last thing I wanted to do was log into the LMS and face more demands. Fatigue made everything harder—I'd read the same paragraph five times without absorbing it. Competing responsibilities meant the LMS often lost out to more immediate needs. It's hard to focus on coursework when you're worried about paying rent.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. Anxiety does that to me—I'm physically present but mentally checked out. Sometimes I'd watch a whole video and realize I hadn't retained anything because I was too stressed about other things. It felt like going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot because they felt like performance—having to sound smart in front of peers. Also avoided starting big assignments until the last minute because the anxiety of beginning was worse than the stress of rushing. I knew it was self-sabotage, but knowing that didn't make it easier to change. The mindfulness stuff helped me recognize these patterns at least.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my LMS usage. I'd be relatively quiet, then spike in activity right before something was due. It wasn't ideal, but it's how I functioned under pressure. The urgency of a deadline cut through my avoidance and forced me to engage. Without that external pressure, I'd drift and procrastinate indefinitely.
Q17: Feedback was a mixed bag for me. Positive feedback motivated me to engage more with that type of work. But critical feedback, even constructive stuff, sometimes made me want to avoid the course entirely for a while. I'd need time to process it before I could use it productively. My resilience isn't great, so setbacks hit harder than they probably should.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming at times with four online courses running simultaneously. When modules were clearly laid out with explicit expectations, I did better. Vague or open-ended structures stressed me out because I never knew if I was doing enough. I needed that clear roadmap to feel like I had any control over the situation.
Q19: Shorter, chunked content was easier to engage with than long lectures or dense readings. Clear rubrics helped a lot—knowing exactly what was expected reduced my anxiety. What made it harder was when deadlines clustered together or when instructions were ambiguous. Also, discussion requirements felt forced sometimes, which made them feel like hoops to jump through rather than genuine learning.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on a bad week—logging in repeatedly out of anxiety, clicking around trying to figure out what to do, but being too overwhelmed to actually complete anything. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes you're just spinning your wheels, stressed out, unable to convert that nervous energy into actual work.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. I ended up with a Merit despite not being super active online because a lot of my learning happened offline. If you're efficient with downloaded materials or learn well through other means, you don't need to be constantly logged in. The grade reflects what you know, not how many times you clicked on something.
Q22: Logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you access, how long you spend. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just staring blankly at a screen. They miss offline study, emotional state, life circumstances, and whether someone is genuinely understanding the material or just going through motions.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine mental effort, not just completing tasks mechanically. It's when the material connects to something meaningful and you want to understand it deeper. For me, engagement feels like curiosity overcoming anxiety—those moments when I'm actually interested rather than just trying to survive the course.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels or how much the mindfulness intervention actually helped me get through tough weeks. It wouldn't capture the offline study sessions, the conversations with friends about course concepts, or the times I was genuinely engaged but not logged in. It also can't show how much I was juggling with limited resources and how that shaped every decision I made about my time.
